docs.title: Next Generation File Format (NGFF)
docs.text: The commonly used term Next Generation File Format (NGFF) refers to the OME-Zarr file format which is a Open Microscopy Environment (OME) community-lead format, for storing and sharing large bioimaging datasets.

docs.title: Comparison of OME-Zarr libs
docs.text: There are a number of python packages providing good support for the OME-Zarr format. See the Comparison of OME-Zarr libs including examples of each of the python packages.

docs.title: An Introduction to OME-Zarr for Big Bioimaging Data
docs.text: The textbook An Introduction to OME-Zarr for Big Bioimaging Data gives a great overview of the NGFF/OME-Zarr format and how to use it in Python. This is using the ome-zarr-models and pydantic-ome-ngff, as well as the core zarr-python package.

docs.title: Persistent metadata
docs.text:
We recommend the use of persistent metadata, together with the image data in dask format. The metadata can be persisted in memory by using Spatial Images which are based on Xarrays.

---
config:
  themeVariables:
    fontSize: 20px
---
flowchart LR
    subgraph data_layers["Persistent metadata"]
        direction TB
        layers("Multiscale Spatial-image<hr><b>Spatial-image
<hr>Xarray<hr>Dask array<hr>File store"):::green
        image("Image data<br>(voxels)"):::blue --> layers
        size("Pixel size<br>(w,h,d)"):::yellow --> layers
        coords("Coordinates<br>(x,y,z)"):::yellow --> layers
        transforms("Transforms<br>[...]"):::yellow --> layers
    end

    %% Styles
    %% classDef default font-size:20
    classDef blue fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#006064
    classDef yellow fill:#fff5e9,stroke:#5e4e20,color:#5e4e20
    classDef green fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#1b5e20

    linkStyle 0 stroke:#006064
    linkStyle 1 stroke:#ddbb00
    linkStyle 2 stroke:#ddbb00
    linkStyle 3 stroke:#ddbb00

The muvis-align project reads OME-Zarr using the ome-zarr-py package, and writes OME-Zarr through multiview-stitcher, which in turn also uses the ome-zarr-py package. It uses persistent metadata together with dask arrays for image data as described before. This metadata is persisted through the OME-Zarr format.

docs.title: Basic example reading OME-Zarr
docs.text:
This is a basic example of reading plain image OME-Zarr data from a remote URL using the ome-zarr python package.

from ome_zarr.io import parse_url
from ome_zarr.reader import Reader

# Example URL of remote data
url = "https://uk1s3.embassy.ebi.ac.uk/idr/zarr/v0.5/idr0062A/6001240_labels.zarr"

# read the image data
reader = Reader(parse_url(url))
# nodes may include images, labels etc
nodes = list(reader())
# first node will be the image pixel data
image_node = nodes[0]

# list of dask arrays at different pyramid levels
data = image_node.data
# dictionary of OME-Zarr metadata
metadata = image_node.metadata

full_size_image_data = data[0]  # access the image data array at full size
axes = "".join([axis["name"] for axis in metadata["axes"]])

docs.title: Basic example writing OME-Zarr
docs.text:
This is a basic example of writing plain image OME-Zarr data to local file using the ome-zarr python package. The zarr file is automatically created with metadata and pyramid scales.

import zarr
from ome_zarr.writer import write_image

path = "path/to/output_image.zarr"

root = zarr.open_group(store=path)
# supports dask data, by default written out at various pyramid sizes
write_image(image=full_size_image_data, group=root, axes=axes)

docs.title: Software development
docs.text: For collaborative software development, we recommend using the following:

docs.title: Platform
docs.text: Use a cloud platform (e.g. GitHub), using repositories for code and documentation, project management and communication. We also recommend using GitHub Issues so users can report issues and bugs, feature-level branching and Pull Requests.

docs.title: Version Control
docs.text: Use version control to track changes, as well as versioning of releases. We recommend using git, and tagging releases with semantic versioning in the format Major.Minor.Patch (a detailed description can be found here).

docs.title: Code Reviews
docs.text: Perform code reviews by a colleague at important stages of development.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: We recommend using markdown files (.md) and automated documentation deployment (e.g. mkdocs) to GitHub Pages. For diagrams we like using mermaid.

docs.title: Comments
docs.text: Comments are an important part of development documentation. Avoid repeating input and output arguments without information beyond the names and types. It's more important to describe the function and to explain why the implementation may have been chosen. Inline comments are useful anywhere where it's not intuitively obvious from code what or why something is done.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: For python testing we recommend using pytest together with tox, and connecting this to a GitHub Action triggered by a push or release.

docs.title: Containerization
docs.text: For reproducibility and ease of installation, we recommend using creating containers (e.g. Docker) to create a consistent environment for users.

docs.title: Support
docs.text: Besides GitHub issues, for further user support we recomment connecting to a single suitable community forum (e.g. image.sc for image analysis related software, and providing a link to this forum in the documentation. This also allows the community to support eachother and share knowledge.
